g.data-narration: Welcome to OS Security in Cloud Computing. This topic covers critical security measures for protecting Operating Systems in cloud environments.
OS Security in cloud involves hardening both Host OS on physical servers and Guest OS inside Virtual Machines, protecting the critical layer between applications and infrastructure.
g.text: What is OS Security in Cloud?
Operating System security in cloud environments refers to hardening and protecting both
Host OS (on physical server) and Guest OS (inside VMs).
The OS sits between applications and hypervisor - making it a prime attack target.

g.data-narration: Under the shared responsibility model in IaaS, the cloud provider secures the Host OS and hypervisor while customers are responsible for securing their Guest OS.
The Host OS has the highest privilege level after the hypervisor. A compromise allows attackers to control ALL tenant VMs on that physical server.
g.text: The Host OS runs directly on the physical server with highest privilege level after hypervisor.

g.data-narration: Host OS hardening includes minimal installation, dedicated purpose, disabling unused services, using MAC like SELinux, file integrity monitoring, and Secure Boot.
Apply security patches promptly using automated tools like WSUS or yum-cron. Test patches in staging before production deployment.
Guest OS security is the customer's responsibility. Key practices include removing unnecessary services, strong authentication, firewalls, and endpoint protection.
Guest OS patching uses automated tools like AWS Systems Manager or Azure Update Management. Immutable infrastructure deploys new VMs from golden images.
Common OS vulnerabilities include privilege escalation, kernel exploits, misconfigured permissions, unpatched software, and default credentials.
Cloud-specific risks include shared kernel in containers, management OS exposure like Dom0 in Xen, and side-channel attacks like Spectre/Meltdown.
g.text: Dom0 in Xen has hypervisor access

g.data-narration: Three main access control models: DAC (flexible Unix permissions), MAC (system-enforced like SELinux), and RBAC (role-based for cloud consoles).
g.text: Access based on roles, not identity

g.data-narration: Process isolation includes virtual memory for separate address spaces, Linux namespaces for container isolation, and cgroups for resource control.
g.text: Each process has separate address space
CPU, Memory, I/O per process group

g.data-narration: OS security requires logging and auditing with tools like auditd and Windows Event Log, centralized collection to SIEM, and tamper-proof storage.
Key takeaways: Host OS compromise affects all VMs, use SELinux/MAC, practice immutable infrastructure, and understand shared kernel risks in containers.
g.text: • Host OS has highest privilege - compromise affects ALL tenant VMs
• Use MAC (SELinux/AppArmor) for system-enforced security policies
• Immutable infrastructure: replace VMs instead of patching live systems
• Cloud risks: shared kernel (containers), management OS, side-channel attacks
• Process isolation: namespaces + cgroups enable container security
